quiz_sets.title: Modern Encryption Basics
quiz_sets.description: Core concepts behind authenticated encryption, symmetric keys, and safe defaults.

questions.prompt: Which option is the safest default for new symmetric file encryption in CryptoLab?
options: AES-CBC without authentication
AES-GCM or ChaCha20-Poly1305
Caesar cipher with a longer key
Base64 with a secret password
questions.explanation: Authenticated encryption modes such as AES-GCM and ChaCha20-Poly1305 protect both confidentiality and integrity.

questions.prompt: What is the main role of a symmetric encryption key?
options: It replaces the need for ciphertext
It is used for both encryption and decryption
It can be published openly
It only verifies message integrity
questions.explanation: Symmetric systems rely on the same secret key to encrypt and decrypt the data.

questions.prompt: Why does CryptoLab show generated AES and ChaCha20 keys in base64?
options: Base64 makes the key stronger
Base64 lets binary key bytes be copied safely as text
Base64 removes the need for decryption settings
Base64 turns encryption into hashing
questions.explanation: The underlying key is still binary. Base64 is just a safe text representation for copying and storing it.

questions.prompt: What should you do immediately after CryptoLab auto-generates an encryption key?
options: Ignore it because the package stores everything needed forever
Save it somewhere secure if you need to decrypt later
Convert it to ROT13
Paste it into the plaintext field
questions.explanation: Without the exact key, decryption will not succeed later.

quiz_sets.title: Hashing And Authentication
quiz_sets.description: Review one-way hashing, MACs, and what counts as integrity protection.

questions.prompt: What makes hashing different from encryption?
options: Hashing is reversible with the correct key
Hashing produces a fixed-size digest and is intended to be one-way
Hashing always uses a public key
Hashing only works on images and files
questions.explanation: A secure hash function is designed to be one-way and to produce a digest rather than recoverable plaintext.

questions.prompt: What problem does HMAC solve compared with a plain hash?
options: It compresses the message better
It adds a shared secret so integrity checks cannot be forged easily
It converts the result into ciphertext
It removes the need for a key
questions.explanation: HMAC combines the message with a shared secret key so an attacker cannot recompute the correct tag without that key.

questions.prompt: Why should MD5 and SHA-1 be avoided for security decisions?
options: They are too slow to compute
They have collision weaknesses and are no longer considered safe
They only work on English text
They are not supported by Python
questions.explanation: Collision attacks make MD5 and SHA-1 unsuitable for modern security-sensitive uses.

questions.prompt: If you only need to verify that a downloaded file has not changed, which choice is most appropriate?
options: A secure hash such as SHA-256
Base64 encoding
Caesar encryption
A random token generator
questions.explanation: A secure hash digest is commonly used to compare file integrity when no shared secret is required.

quiz_sets.title: Classical Ciphers And Encoding
quiz_sets.description: Separate historical ciphers from simple encoding tools and know when neither provides real security.

questions.prompt: What is the main reason Base64 should not be treated as encryption?
options: It changes the alphabet size
It is trivially reversible and provides no secrecy
It only works on short strings
It requires a 256-bit key
questions.explanation: Encoding changes representation, not security. Anyone can decode Base64 without a secret.

questions.prompt: Why are classical ciphers like Caesar and Vigenere included in CryptoLab?
options: They are recommended for production systems
They are educational examples of how cryptography evolved
They replace modern authenticated encryption
They store file metadata better
questions.explanation: Classical ciphers are useful for learning historical ideas, but they are not safe for real-world protection.

questions.prompt: Which statement about ROT13 is true?
options: It is an AEAD mode
It is a fixed Caesar-style transformation and not real security
It requires a random nonce
It is stronger than AES-256
questions.explanation: ROT13 is a simple reversible substitution and is included only as a basic encoding-style transform.

questions.prompt: What kind of attack breaks monoalphabetic ciphers easily when enough ciphertext is available?
options: Frequency analysis
Quantum tunneling
Binary decoding
Image compression
questions.explanation: Monoalphabetic ciphers preserve letter-frequency patterns, which makes them vulnerable to frequency analysis.
